issues.title: Add outline of Security Considerations Topics/sub-sections
issues.author: rahelFain
issues.body:
Provide an outline of the topics that will be discussed in this document. 
This could also include a statement noting that use case specific security considerations will need to be specified in their protocol specification document(s).

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I began thinking of a few topics that could be included in the Security Considerations Section - but have listed them in this issue rather than a PR b/c I still have yet to discuss these with my co-authors. 

Potential Entries subsections to include:

1. Digital Emblems do not actively stop/defend against attacks: For emblems that indicate an asset's protected status, digital emblems can at best serve as a deterrent for attack - as there is no guarantee a would-be attacker will respect the laws, agreements or standards granting the asset its status. 

2. Plausible deniability surrounding whether or not emblem was seen, visible or verifiable at time of validation.

Note: This is by no means intended to be an exhaustive list of topics but rather a first brainstorm.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: 3. For Digital Emblems that require Authentication of the Digital Emblem, origin authentication or other authentication of the Bearer, or Authorization of the Digital Emblem (the MUSTs in Section 3),  the threat model addressed is presentation of falsified Digital Emblems. Both 1 and 2 above will require addressing this threat model.

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body: Several of the use cases would probably want to plan for forensics capabilities, such as a history of creation and modification of an emblem, associating it with internal references for identity of the creator/modifier, etc. This is not outward security, but merits some thinking about how external or encrypted-in-place metadata can be associated with an emblem.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body: Partially addressed in #7

comments.author: wnunezc
comments.body:
@rahelFain Speaking in my personal capacity.

From the perspective of IHL-related use cases, I think the Security Considerations section could benefit from a short outline that separates different kinds of risk.

In particular, it may be useful to distinguish between:

- **False or unauthorized claims of protected status.** A digital emblem or marking should not make it easy for an unauthorized actor to claim a protected status merely by publishing data.

- **Over-reliance on the emblem.** A digital emblem does not itself create legal protection or actively defend an asset. It can make a claimed or authorized status visible and technically verifiable, but downstream legal or operational interpretation remains outside the protocol.

- **Visibility versus exposure.** For some protected assets, visibility is part of the protective function. For others, publishing too much information may increase risk to the asset, staff, infrastructure, or affected population.

- **Undetectable validation.** Some use cases, especially those involving protected assets in armed conflict, may require that discovery or validation not alert the asset operator or other observers.

- **Authorization and revocation.** For legally-derived use cases, validators may need to distinguish between data that is merely self-published and data that is issued or authorized under an applicable trust model.

- **Misuse and probing.** Adverse actors may ignore, falsify, misuse, or probe digital emblems. The draft does not need to model every attacker behavior, but the security considerations could acknowledge these categories when deriving requirements.

- **Proof, audit, or forensic extensions.** Some use cases may need evidence that an emblem existed, was discoverable, or was validated at a relevant time. However, those mechanisms may be profile-specific or extension-specific and should be considered carefully against privacy and undetectable-validation requirements.

I do not think the requirements document needs to resolve all of these issues in detail. But a short outline could help clarify which risks belong to the core DIEM architecture and which risks should be addressed by individual profiles, extensions, or downstream operational/legal processes.

comments.author: ts5746
comments.body:
As a suggestion for accountability or forensic use (probably need to find the proper terminology here), one approach could be to define different levels of proof to support different use cases and implementations, e.g.:

1. Could an actor (that is held accountable) have obtained the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that the emblem was available/discoverable and verifiable for an actor at some relevant moment in time?

2. Did the actor obtain the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that an actor actually discovered/accessed a verifiable emblem at a certain relevant moment in time?

3. Did the actor correctly validate the emblem, i.e. can it be proven that an actor actually validated the emblem correctly?
 
For example, under IHL, armed forces have an obligation to make an effort to adhere to the principles of proportionality and distinction, and to the prohibition on causing unnecessary suffering. Therefore, the weaker proof (level 1) might be sufficient in certain cases, if it can be proven the emblem existed and was (easily) accessible/obtainable for anyone, regardless of whether the actor actually did obtain the emblem.

Also, level 2/3 might not be compatible with use cases where discovery and validation must be undetected, but very reasonable or even required for other cases.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
@ts5746 Thank you for the suggestion! 
I've created [a PR with some initial changes](https://github.com/ietf-wg-diem/diem-requirements/pull/25) based on it.

issues.title: Attacker and authorizing party roles and operations in use cases
issues.author: dkg
issues.body:
When describing a use case for a digital protocol, i'm used to seeing use cases presented from every party involved in a transaction.

For example, in digital id discussions, we need to talk about how a verifier operates, as well as how an issuer operates, and how a holder operates.

The use cases i'm seeing in the current document are all split out by talking about different bearer scenarios.

But i don't see any discussion of an attacker's perspective, or an authorizing party's perspective.  Should we detail those perspectives within each use case?  or should we detail them as their own distinct use case?

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
Thanks for suggesting that, dkg! What you raise here mirrors some issues I noticed when talking about the architecture to Jim on the hallway.

I think we should add those details to each use cases, but we should consider group very similar use cases together, and generalizing where possible. Depending on the feedback on individual use cases (e.g., press use case mostly received negative feedback), we might also save ourselves some work for use cases that we anticipate will not be in a WGLC draft.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body:
Below is some potential text, which doesn't have requirements language yet.  It was written a while back as part of research at my org.  We are discussing DIEM now with a couple of potential contributors (or at least quiet stakeholders) from foreign offices. 

Digital emblems can also protect diplomatic pouch shipments, diplomatic couriers, and diplomatic envoys, who are protected under the 1961 Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations, which states that they may not be stopped, delayed, or inspected. This creates the paradox that the validity of their credentials must be evaluated, yet doing so has historically compromised the very rights that are intended to be signaled. Diplomatic markings have also been misappropriated as cover for the smuggling of drugs and other contraband. Digital emblems, which can be validated instantaneously, at a distance, and without interrupting the subject, solve both of these problems, while streamlining and automating customs and immigrations processes.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body:
> (or at least quiet stakeholders)

By this I mean one or more individuals who consent to be acknowledged.

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: @mcr, right now, an expert inspection of external physical wrappings is used to validate that it's a diplomatic wrapping (to greatly over-simplify).  A digital component in the wrapping (preceded by a non-technical process that manages how it is or isn't enclosed in the wrapping) could provide a link out to DE and be able to be validated. This isn't much different from processes still to be defined under IHL use cases that permit/deny authorization to publish a DE.

comments.author: tojens-ietf
comments.body:
_(speaking as an individual, no hat)_

> Feel free to complain to the IESG that they did not charter the full original scope if you would like.

We all chartered that together, let's not pin the blame on the IESG of the time. For more concrete guidance, I would turn to WG consensus [from a previous meeting](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/minutes-123-diem-202507221500/) that told the editors to "concentrate discussion on requirements covered by the initial scope" but not forbidding "inclusion of out-of-scope
requirements which are non controversial". 

That said, for this specific use case, I used [this text](https://www.lawglobalhub.com/article-27-vienna-convention-on-diplomatic-relations-1961/) since the original link wasn't loading for me. From this, I derive this is indeed a case where an emblem makes sense, and a digital emblem could offer additional benefits (e.g. making it removable). 

That said, what is unique about this case is about how _humans_ behave (e.g. cannot open the bag) not how _emblems_ behave. Looking through our existing requirements, I do not see any missing for implementing this one, which seems pretty straightforward using Discovery, Removable, and Validation requirement subsections, along with the MAY for including additional data with the emblem (such as "indicating [the courier's] status and the number of packages constituting the diplomatic bag", from Article 27 subsection 5). Even with proximity discovery being beyond scope, I could imagine use in the opposite direction (email from one embassy to another: "diplomat identified by <domain-name> is headed your way" and we stay out of the way telling anybody how to bind domain names physically to somebody/something).

Does anyone else see a requirement currently missing this would need to have listed? My point being this document is **not** saying "only included use cases matter" but to use use cases we have active engagement from stakeholders on to help us validate design against requirements. We absolutely should hope that fulfilling their requirements will then make the mechanisms developed here usable by any other use cases with a subset of the same requirements. If there is a missing requirement, let's focus on that for guiding drafted text (right now I wouldn't know what to suggest that would expand the requirements list).

issues.title: ISO shipping containers and related systems
issues.author: ohmantics
issues.body:
Early pre-WG use cases discussions included ISO shipping containers. They're covered by ISO 6346 with owner codes registered with [Bureau International des Containers](https://bic-code.org). The International Convention for Safe Containers established the [CSC Combined Data Plate](https://www.bic-code.org/csc-combined-data-plate/) which labels them for safety. Those come from the UN and International Maritime Organization.

https://www.bic-code.org/marking-of-containers/ goes into more detail about the ISO codes, CSC Combined Data Plate and includes an existing geofencing database used by IoT-enabled containers including a facility coding system established in ISO 9897, which itself is a coding system to describe the condition of a container including damage.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
1. Are these containers in motion, or ones sitting in some compound?
2. RFC7744.  The container is not the important part.  It's the communication to the tracking system, and the tracking system.
3. If the goal is to prevent digital attack on containers en-route, then the emblem will wind up applying to the ship.

I would make it out of scope.

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body:
> 3\. If the goal is to prevent digital attack on containers en-route, then the emblem will wind up applying to the ship.

The ship would have an emblem bundling all the emblems of its manifest.

issues.title: How should this draft expand on the Press case?
issues.author: amankin
issues.body:
The draft currently has a placeholder:

   Journalists in conflict zones use protective markings that indicate
   their status as a non-combatant.  Digital assets belonging to the
   press could be digitally marked, and protective markings in conflict
   zones could be digitized.

This does not even say clearly something that was articulated at IETF 124 by a stakeholder there
are distinct use cases here:  1. a digital emblem form of the PRESS marking worn by a person to mark
being a non-combatant; 2. a digital emblem marking on equipment (whether it about networked
computers/databases etc or about physical cameras, computers, sound equipment etc).  Let's use comments 
on this issue to flesh out 1. and 2. and also to reflect by the stakeholders about how much to develop these 
two sub-use-cases.

comments.author: tojens-ietf
comments.body:
_(speaking as an individual, no hat)_

> This does not even say clearly something that was articulated at IETF 124 by a stakeholder

The comment made by Cory at IETF 124 was that "we are not opposed to the Press use case being listed here. We do not see a concrete use case for it that we are pursuing" ([meeting recording at timestamp 1:35:07](https://youtu.be/Q17UfUwzBz8?si=_4IEwjk9bQJIXDAs&t=5707)). I do not see other comments from a press use case stakeholder during that meeting, though I may have just missed them on re-review.

The comments made by Chantel at IETF 123 in a back and forth discussion with Rohan were, and I'm paraphrasing, that she has reservations about its use at all given it may invite more attacks versus trying to stay unnoticed ([discussion begins at 41:35](https://youtu.be/qNelwWBU11Y?si=2tKOgQmu7nRulhlC&t=2495)).

I agree with you and Chantel who both said that "the press" is not a monolithic organization with singular needs (my paraphrase). One organization not pursuing a use case doesn't mean there couldn't be others. That said, it's difficult to validate text with stakeholders if they are not here with an interest in doing so, or even go so far as to express misgivings in the absence of several press reviewers (near the end of Chantel's remarks at the mic line).

As I am asking with every other use case: are there requirements that do not yet exist in the draft that need to for the use case you are envisioning? At IETF 123, the editors presented a slide breaking down the matrix of use cases versus requirements that compared a few other use cases against the press use case ([see slides 13 and 14](https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/123/materials/slides-123-diem-use-cases-and-requirements-00)). From this, I do not see the press case needing to be expanded in the use cases document to add new requirements, so I don't think in light of the comments brought to the IETF since the WG was formed that expanding on this text is a good idea.

issues.title: Should we add CITES as a digital emblem use case?
issues.author: rohanmahy
issues.body: CITES: the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora  includes a system for tracking the legal movement of endangered species. If we can get stakeholder involvement I think this would be another good use case.

comments.author: tojens-ietf
comments.body:
_(speaking as an individual, no hat)_

Do we expect that will expand the existing requirements list? I think that should be the guiding principle at this point. The important point of this document is to create the set of requirements the architecture doc needs to account for using use cases to illustrate why with active stakeholders. I want to make sure we don't fall into the trap of the reverse: making this a never-ending list of active or potential stakeholders when the requirements as written would work for them whether their use case is listed or not. 

Personally, I do not see this introducing anything beyond what is in there or what is out of current charter scope (which the WG decided should not be the focus of the editors).

comments.author: rohanmahy
comments.body:
> _(speaking as an individual, no hat)_
> 
> Do we expect that will expand the existing requirements list? I think that should be the guiding principle at this point. The important point of this document is to create the set of requirements the architecture doc needs to account for using use cases to illustrate why with active stakeholders. I want to make sure we don't fall into the trap of the reverse: making this a never-ending list of active or potential stakeholders when the requirements as written would work for them whether their use case is listed or not.
> 
> Personally, I do not see this introducing anything beyond what is in there or what is out of current charter scope (which the WG decided should not be the focus of the editors).

I think that you raised a good point that we not hold the train for new use cases with identical requirements.

Where I found this use case interesting was that the treatment could be effectively the opposite of the Diplomatic pouch use case. You MUST NOT open this shipment vs you MUST open and inspect this shipment to make sure it is what is described (so someone doesn't replace a rare parrot with a chicken or ship five instead of three of them).

(chair hat off)

comments.author: tojens-ietf
comments.body:
_(speaking as an individual, no hat)_

I was thinking about something similar on the other comment. It's true that the use cases are different for the humans interpreting the emblems, but I don't see how they are different for the implementors of resulting standards. We aren't in the business of telling implementors how to honor the _intent_ communicated by the emblems (we better _never_ write an RFC that says "observers of a digital Red Cross MUST NOT send more than 50 megabits per second of traffic..." or anything like that). We just want to explain how to detect and verify emblems, then leave acting on the detected and verified emblems up to the parties to do whatever they were going to do about it.

Or am I missing something? Should there be a technical difference in the way emblems are implemented for use cases that mandate interaction with the asset versus forbid interaction?

Or alternatively, do you see value in using this as a shim example illustrating how these requirements are extensible far beyond the examples we end up including?

issues.title: Should whether an emblem supports or mandates forensic tracing be an extension or core consideration?
issues.author: rahelFain
issues.body:
2. I see the forensic tracking as a core requirement. That is to say, I don't think all use cases will need to support forensic tracing of emblem issuance/discoverability etc. but I think that every emblem's depiction should indicate whether or not it has an associated forensic tracing mechanism or ledger - and in cases where it does should include pointers to it.

_Originally posted by @rahelFain in https://github.com/ietf-wg-diem/diem-requirements/issues/25#issuecomment-3961344021_

2a. In addition to noting this as a core field, I think the question of whether or not a specific use case supports and/or requires the use of one or more forensic tracing mechanisms and the reasoning behind the decision(s) reached needs to be detailed in its security considerations. In cases where forensic tracing is optional I think the security considerations will also need to detail the risks vs. benefits of including it within the emblem

comments.author: tojens-ietf
comments.body:
_(speaking as an individual, no hat)_

I think it should be defined as a possible extension. I can imagine it being useful, but I think requiring every other use of emblems to have to specify they do not in fact have an associated ledger is wasteful. There is already a requirement for emblems MAY having associated data. I think any use of that associated data should be viewed as an extension. That extension defining how a ledger is presented will allow implementations that check for ledgers identify when one is absent, and allow implementations that do not care about ledgers to not have to have parsing code to check for something they don't care about.

> [...] needs to be detailed in its security considerations [...]

Completely agree.

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body: I think forensic tracing establishes a need for "selective disclosure" as a requirement. This means that sub-dictionary portions of the emblem may be encrypted for specific viewers as they would contain 'internal' references such as an employee ID of the person who authorized the creation of the emblem record.

comments.author: wnunezc

issues.title: Additional potential emblems: Any missing requirements?
issues.author: rohanmahy
issues.body:
In chat during IETF 125, @ohmantics mentioned several additional potential emblem types. Please let us know if we are missing any requirements needed for these use cases.


> For consideration, here are a list of use cases we haven't discussed:
> 
> Wassenaar Arrangement / Export Controls — dual-use goods and technologies have export control markings; digital emblems could mark shipments or data transfers subject to export control regimes
> Antarctic Treaty System — research stations and vessels in Antarctica have protected status; digital assets of Antarctic research programs could benefit from emblems
> Outer Space Treaty / COPUOS — space objects, ground stations, and telemetry systems have registration and non-interference obligations; satellite ground infrastructure could bear digital emblems
> Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons (CCW) — minefields and areas with unexploded ordnance carry physical markings; digital geodata could carry equivalent emblems
> International Telecommunication Union (ITU) — frequency allocations and emergency telecommunications have protected status under the ITU Constitution; digital infrastructure supporting emergency comms could be marked
> 
> Undersea cable systems — submarine cables have protected status under UNCLOS and the 1884 Convention for the Protection of Submarine Telegraph Cables; landing stations, management systems, and monitoring infrastructure could bear digital emblems
> Critical infrastructure / CNI designations — many nations designate critical national infrastructure; while not treaty-based, mutual recognition frameworks could use digital emblems
> Election infrastructure — some jurisdictions designate election systems as critical infrastructure; digital emblems could signal this status to potential attackers
> Maritime vessels (IMO) — beyond containers, vessels themselves carry IMO numbers and safety certifications (SOLAS); their digital systems (AIS, ECDIS) could bear emblems
> Pharmaceutical supply chain (WHO prequalification) — beyond WHO's Red Cross-like protection, WHO-prequalified medicines in transit could use digital emblems for anti-counterfeiting

comments.author: ohmantics
comments.body:
I’m not an expert in these use cases; they came up in an unrelated search effort.

In the mental model I’ve posted, I believe that these use cases are mostly covered by existing requirements, including “selective disclosure” of portions of the dictionary. Perhaps some of these would need some kind of additional root of trust on top of a domain owner to tie the domain to authority from a central organization. I think that could be handled by application of a certificate/signature record on the domain that is signed by the central authority.

pulls.title: Add charter definitions
pulls.author: rahelFain
pulls.body: Explicitly adds the definitions for terms "(digital) emblem," bearer" and "validations" from the current charter.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body: Response to feedback that the definitions in the charter could change in the event that the DIEM WG is rechartered sometime in the future.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
Thanks for preparing, @rahelFain, and iterating, @rohanmahy!

I must say, I don't like the "definition" of (digital) emblems in the charter. I don't find it to define anything. In the charter, this is not a problem, because we don't say: "Look, here we define what an emblem is." It's informal and glue text. This changes, however, if we copy-paste these passages into the document.

Let's look at what the definition says from a more abstract point of view:

> Emblems such as [...] can be [X]. Emblems can also be [Y].

The three sentences after that are more a problem statement that I would rather keep in the charter. These two sentences don't define anything, they only give examples of emblems.

Personally, I have two, contradictory goals:

- Have unambiguous terminology.
- Don't spend much time discussing what "digital emblems" are.

We already spent a long time during chartering trying to define digital emblems, and my feeling is that little came out of that. And I would rather sacrifice ambiguous terminology than having to spent a long time defining digital emblems.

What do you think? Is it really necessary to have a definition of digital emblems that encompass everything ever brought to the WG?

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
@felixlinker My intent in adding the definition text was not necessarily to state that this was the official definition, but rather to have the referenced text explicitly copied here so that it could be referenced even if the charter were to change. (Hence the comments noting that the text is copy-pasted from the current version of the charter.) 
I'd be willing to call this something less firm than a set of definitions (say for example descriptions) and to add a caveat that later documents may more precisely define these terms - and that the sole reason for including this text was to ensure document clarity.

pulls.title: Refactor use cases section
pulls.author: felixlinker
pulls.body:
This PR depends on #20.

Two thoughts were expressed by different participants:

1. We should list as many use cases as possible.
2. We should be clear which use cases has received sufficient stake holder input, in particular, when it comes to evaluating whether its requirements are accurate and there is interest in deployment.

This PR splits the use cases section in two: One for use cases with stakeholder evaluation, and one for use cases for future reference. This split allows us to track which use case received evaluation of its requirements, but also allows us to keep use cases for future reference, as well as be explicit about what we aim to target for the extensibility requirement.

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I wanted to be sure to follow up on this PR, since I have been looking it over and thinking about what changes I think still need to be made. 
For the time being, I figured I'd share my thoughts. 
Overall, these changes largely seem to center on the balance between the inclusion of use cases where we are unable to get stakeholder input, or where stakeholder input is only obtained after this document has reached WG last call. 
Looking over the minutes from our previous meetings, particularly those from [IETF 123](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/minutes-123-diem-202507221500/), I do see that the majority of those who voted in the show of hands indicated that those use cases can be excluded (29 for, 13 against, 2 no opinion, 25 present but did not vote). 

However, the subsequent discussion raised the point that 
 If a stakeholder comes at a later time (e.g. after the document has reached WG last call), we cannot say "too late." This is particularly important as some stakeholders are very difficult to find and/or get ahold of. (In some cases merely identifying who the right stakeholders are  can prove challenging.)

Ultimately, the chairs determined they would need to work on a way to get more stakeholders involved. 
However, I do not remember us ever reaching any sort of consensus on how to handle cases where one or more stakeholders give us input on their (respective) use case's requirements after this document has been completed/has reached WG last call. 

To this end, my best suggestion is that we first introduce all of the use cases and later on go into more detail about the extent of stakeholder input we have received as of this writing as well as more detailed descriptions of the use cases for which we have received more stakeholder input. 
I also think we need to devise a system through which input from stakeholders who join the conversation at a later date can still be noted and taken into account. 

Coming back to the revisions at hand: What I am really wary of is listing the use cases for which we have received input first or putting more emphasis on these from the get-go (either explicitly or implicitly) because to the best of my understanding, the WG must not/ is expressly not allowed to state or imply that any use case is better or more worthy of being considered than another. 
To that end, I like Allison's suggestion of including a matrix indicating the level of stakeholder input received as of this document's writing - perhaps as a way to introduce the more detailed accounts of the different use cases.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
You write "the WG must not/ is expressly not allowed to state or imply that any use case is better or more worthy of being considered than another." Where does that come from? My concern is that we *cannot* address certain use cases because they're underspecified. How are we supposed to account for the "United Nations Peacekeepers" use case if all we have in writing on it is: "UN Peacekeepers use protective markings in theater as well as facilities associated with the mission." How does that inform protocol development? How does this use case relate to our requirements and domain model?

Your concern seems to be that use cases may receive stakeholder evaluation after a WGLC. I think we can mitigate that risk easily because the WG is always free to change course. Should a stakeholder join the discussion one year into protocol development, we can simply write a new document that details that use case and have it adopted by the WG. So I don't see any risks here!

comments.author: rahelFain
comments.body:
I want to make a distinction between saying a use case is more worthy of being considered for implementation and whether or not it's feasible for a use case to be implemented at a given time.  

As I understand it, the intent is for this document to be finalized upon reaching WG last call. This means that any statements about which use case is more worthy of being considered or implemented would outlast any changes in the WG's scope, and would therefore effectively be value statements saying _use case x is the more important or the most important_ regardless of what future information we may obtain surrounding the needs motivating other use cases and  the respective impacts of standardization of digital emblems for them. 

Think for example, if we had initially gotten extensive input and interest for the RAMSAR use case and less for that of ICRC one and therefore failed to mention the ICRC use case in the primary use cases and/or limited its mention to a single sentence in an appendix.  It seems pretty clear how these actions could convey an implicit message that the RAMSAR use case is in an absolute sense, a better use case, is more worthy of implementation than the ICRC's etc..
Given the extent of what we do know about the ICRC's pressing need for digital emblems, their interest in its standardization/development and the impact it stands to have, it seems pretty clear how conveying that sort of message would not only be unwise but also inflammatory and would constitute us overstepping our role as technologists. 

To be clear, I am not saying the WG cannot or should not decide to first define or implement digital emblems for the use cases for which we currently have more stakeholder input or involvement and a better defined set of requirements. (After all it will be more feasible to do this in these cases.) All I am saying is that that decision should be independent of the extent to which we mention the other previously identified use cases in this document.

comments.author: felixlinker
comments.body:
I totally get your point on distinguishing use cases "more" and "less" worthy, which is why I changed the language on that. Now, the section only says that one section contains use cases that have been evaluated and use cases that have not.

But I also understand the WGLC, document writing process, and WG scope differently. My understanding is that you can always change scope, write new documents, etc. After WGLC, *this* document is finished, but I think nothing would change the WG to write a new document that lists additional use cases. Think about how many RFCs have been amended/superseded. I don't think there will be any issues with use cases that receive their evaluation after WGLC. But I will confirm my understanding with the chairs!

comments.author: wnunezc
comments.body:
# Proof of Presence: Evidentiary Concepts and Technical Validation Flows

Thank you for this PR. I think the discussion would benefit from separating several different evidentiary concepts that may otherwise be grouped under “proof of presence”.

From a technical and legal/operational perspective, these seem to be different questions:

1. **Proof of existence**  
   Can it be established that DIEM data or an emblem record existed for asset X at time T?

2. **Proof of discoverability**  
   Can it be established that a DIEM-aware client could have discovered the emblem record through the expected discovery mechanism at time T?

3. **Proof of access or query**  
   Can it be established that a specific querying party queried the emblem or discovery mechanism?

4. **Proof of validation**  
   Can it be established that a specific querying party successfully validated the emblem object or binding?

5. **Proof of response content**  
   Can it be established what response or emblem content was returned to a querying party at time T?

6. **Proof of subsequent conduct**  
   Can it be established what the querying party did or did not do after validation?

These are related, but they are not the same. In particular, proof of validation does not necessarily prove subsequent lawful conduct. It may show that a party validated an emblem, but it does not prove that the party did nothing else unless combined with other mechanisms such as tamper-evident seals, chain-of-custody records, logs, attestations, or other forensic systems.

It may also be useful to state that some of these evidentiary goals can conflict with undetectable validation. For example, proof of access or proof of validation by a specific querying party may require logging or attestation, while undetectable validation may require that the bearer of the emblem cannot observe who validated it.

Therefore, perhaps the draft could avoid treating “proof of presence” as a single concept and instead define which evidentiary property is required by each use case, who needs it, and what trade-offs it creates.

## Physical Recognition vs. Digital Validation

I also think the “proof of presence” discussion may benefit from a clearer separation between physical recognition and digital validation.

In the physical world, a protective emblem or distinctive sign is usually visible without interaction. For example, an observer can see a Red Cross emblem on a medical facility, a Blue Shield on cultural property, or the special sign for works containing dangerous forces, without the marked object knowing who observed it. The observer remains anonymous unless some additional interaction occurs.

In the digital environment, this changes. If proof of presence or validation requires querying an endpoint operated by the bearer of the emblem, by a State, by an issuing authority, or by another registry, then the act of validation itself may reveal metadata such as source IP address, timing, frequency, geography, or operational interest. This may conflict with undetectable validation.

Therefore, it may be useful to distinguish several technical flows.

## Possible Technical Flows

### 1. Direct Bearer Validation

A DIEM-aware client queries the protected asset directly.

```text
Client -> protected asset / bearer endpoint -> DIEM response
```

This is simple, but it is detectable by the bearer. The protected asset may learn who queried, when, and from where.

### 2. Issuer or Authority Validation

A DIEM-aware client queries an authority endpoint.

```text
Client -> issuing authority endpoint -> validation response
```

This avoids contacting the protected asset directly, but the issuing authority may still learn who is validating which asset.

### 3. Cached or Replicated Validation

A signed DIEM object is replicated through caches, resolvers, mirrors, or transparency-style distribution.

```text
Issuer signs DIEM object
        |
        v
Public cache / resolver / mirror
        |
        v
Client validates signature locally
```

This allows the client to validate without contacting the protected asset and without necessarily contacting the issuer at validation time.

### 4. Offline Validation Bundle

The client receives a periodically updated signed dataset or bundle.

```text
Issuer/neutral registry -> signed bundle -> client
Client validates locally
```

This supports undetectable validation, because the client does not need to reveal which specific asset it is validating at the moment of use.

### 5. Privacy-Preserving Query Model

A neutral or trusted service could answer validation queries without learning the exact asset being queried, or without linking the query to the requester, using privacy-preserving mechanisms.

Possible mechanisms could include:

- hash-based lookup with salted or structured identifiers;
- signed inclusion proofs;
- transparency logs or Merkle-tree inclusion proofs;
- private set membership techniques;
- blind signatures or anonymous tokens;
- resolver-side caching;
- OCSP-stapling-like approaches, where the bearer presents a fresh signed status without the validator querying the authority;
- CRLite-like approaches, where clients download compressed validity/revocation data and validate locally.

## Abstract Flow

A possible abstract flow could be:

```text
1. A competent authority or issuer authorizes a protected-status marking.
2. The issuer signs a DIEM object for asset X.
3. The signed object or its digest is published through DNS, a registry,
   a transparency log, or a replicated cache.
4. A DIEM-aware client discovers that DIEM data exists for asset X.
5. The client obtains the signed object or an inclusion proof from a cache,
   bundle, or privacy-preserving service.
6. The client validates locally:
   - asset binding;
   - profile;
   - issuer;
   - signature;
   - validity period;
   - revocation or status;
   - applicable proof or inclusion evidence.
7. The client can determine that the marking existed or was discoverable
   at time T without necessarily revealing the validation event to the bearer.
```

## Suggested Clarification for the Draft

This suggests that “proof of presence” may need to be decomposed into different evidentiary properties:

- proof that a DIEM object existed at time T;
- proof that it was discoverable through the expected mechanism;
- proof that a specific response was available;
- proof that a client queried or accessed it;
- proof that a client successfully validated it;
- proof that the client later acted or did not act in a certain way.

These are related, but they are not the same. Some of them may be compatible with undetectable validation, while others may not.

For example, proving that a DIEM object existed in a signed transparency log at time T may be compatible with undetectable validation. But proving that a specific military or CERT client queried the object at time T may require logging or attestation, which conflicts with undetectable validation.

For IHL-related use cases, this distinction may be important because physical emblem recognition is normally passive and anonymous. A digital equivalent should avoid turning every validation attempt into a detectable visit whenever the use case requires undetectable validation.

So perhaps the draft could clarify that proof of presence should not be treated as a single property. Each use case should define:

- who needs the proof;
- what exactly must be proven;
- whether validation must be undetectable;
- whether the proof is about existence, discoverability, access, validation, response content, or subsequent conduct;
- and which publication or validation model is acceptable for that use case.

reviews.author: felixlinker
reviews.body: No strong opinion on these changes, but I see some technical issues in how the requirements interact with the use case.

comments.body: Editing nit: I would move this text into `#diplo-pouch` and simply forward ref here. It's good to have use cases descriptions be self-contained.

I can see how level 3 can prove that an agent inspected a digital emblem. But I see two problems in how the requirements interact with the use case. Maybe, both can be addressed.

1. Is it mandatory for bags to be inspected? If yes, then indeed, you can verify that procedure was followed by checking that when a bag crosses a border, its emblem was validated. If checking is not mandatory, there is little the emblem proof of access proves, right?
2. I do not see how proof of access proves that the agent did "nothing else." That would require some sort of seal of the bag, i.e., opening the bag destroys the emblem.

Those are both fair points. My plan is to update the text to effectively give the two answers below.

1. Since the emblem denotes that the contents of the pouch are exempt from the normal point of entry customs inspections and restrictions, it is mandatory for the point of entry customs officials to validate the emblem.  However, in the case that the emblem cannot be validated it may be acceptable to use physical markings that could be validated.  - That is to say that the validation of the digital emblem failing may not give the customs officials permission to open or inspect the pouch. Rather, that validation of a diplomatic pouch's digital or physical emblem succeeding indicates it enjoys the treaty's guaranteed protections.

2. Proof of access does not prove that the agent did "nothing else." 
In all of the discussions I've had around this, my sense has been that mechanisms for guaranteeing "nothing else" was done is beyond the scope of what the emblem itself may be able to guarantee. To that end, the Level 3 validation may need to be part of an attested chain of custody (likely some form of SCITT record set or ledger) and/or accompanied by some physical mechanisms for tamper-proofing the pouch/making it impossible to open the pouch, x-ray it etc. without detection or the generation of forensic evidence.

Okay, I see that you now list in the limitations that it actually may not be possible to verify that "little or nothing else" was done, but why still list it here then as part of level 3 proof of presence?
What is a proof of presence record?

Based on my understanding of your question, my best answer is that the record is a transcript of the proof... 
Thinking about this I can see that there is a lack of clarity here. Within the confines of this example, I need to distinguish between:
- proving the pouch's digital emblem existed/was accessible at the time of the pouch's arrival 
- proving that customs agents performed the requisite checks of the emblem. 

The Baseline proof of presence would largely be the first thing. 
This would then need to be accompanied by the signed validation attempt and its result to establish the second.

Edit to note that I've made these changes.

I was trying to get at the fact that there was still value in establishing that the a digital emblem for the pouch existed and that the validation occurred even if no further protections or checks are used. This option may be chosen in cases where there was less concern about the risk posed by inspection or where further chain of custody and/or anti-tampering mechanisms were not feasible (e.g. possibly due to the size/weight of the contents, a lack of infrastructure or resources to support them, etc.). 

Would this be addressed by adjusting the phrasing to say the following?:
`That is to say that they validated the pouch's digital emblem and can reasonably be assumed to have done little to nothing else.`

```suggestion
Since emblems themselves are unable to directly protect assets against attack, emblems indicating assets are entitled to protections may require a mechanism through which violations of their laws or provisions can be verified forensically.
This would be particularly relevant in cases where emblems can be applied and removed dynamically.
These protections are defined in three different levels, listed from weakest to strongest.
```

@rahelFain do you want to explain that Level 3 would only possible for the querying party (how could I tell if you verified a claim or not?), whereas Levels 1 and 2 could be proven by either party depending on the design.
The querying party showing it accessed the emblem (or that definitively no emblem was present) might be a different requirement than the queried party showing that querying party asked for and received an emblem.

```suggestion
Level 3 -  presence, verifiability access and verification: Demonstrating presence verifiability and access and that the querying party verified the emblem upon accessing it. This level of proof can only be made by the querying party.
```

```suggestion
Digital emblems can protect diplomatic pouch shipments, diplomatic couriers, and diplomatic envoys.
All three of these are protected
```

```suggestion
instantaneously, at a distance, and without interrupting the subject, address both of these problems, while streamlining
```
What about the pouch itself?

```suggestion
As noted in Section {{proof-pres}}, a Level 3 Proof of presence record could help demonstrate, for the benefit of the customs service, that a customs agent(s) in a Point of Entry country validated the diplomatic pouch.
To that end, the Proof of Presence record of processing a diplomatic pouch's digital emblem could include the following information.
```

```suggestion
As indicated in Section {{proof-pres}}, Level 3 Proof of Presence alone does not provide proof that no tampering with the diplomatic pouch or inspection of its contents has occurred.
This is because a proof of presence neither provides a chain of custody nor any mechanisms to detect tampering should it occur.
For this reason, Level 3 validation may be used along side or as part of an attested chain of custody and/or accompanied by the use of physical mechanisms for tamper-proofing a physical asset.
Any such chain of custody specification or anti-tampering mechanism is out of the scope of the DIEM WG.
```

Just a small nit that Level 2 could be available for the querying party without contradicting any Undetectable Validation requirement, but having queried party Level 2 would contradict any Undetectable Validation requirement.
I'd be fine with adding a footnote to that effect, if it seems necessary.

pulls.title: Expand requirements to flexibly account for less-understood use cases
pulls.author: tojens-ietf
pulls.body:
This requirement expansion resulted from brainstorming with Rohan about the use cases that aren't well represented in WG participation but have been brought up as potential future uses of Digital Emblems.

I believe with such an expansion, the continued expansion of use cases becomes unnecessary given this seems to account for the things required by the beyond-initial-scope cases without having to go through the controversy of debating appropriateness of each of these individually. The intention is to ensure the requirements on the architecture document specifically permit the flexibility needed for Deliverable 3 documents (specific digital emblem standards) to do whatever they need to without having to support arbitrary requirements of one another.

@ohmantics @amankin  I especially want to hear your take as people passionately exploring other use cases.

FYI @rohanmahy

comments.author: amankin
comments.body: I don't have anything to click to show I approve the PR,  so take this comment as recording my approval.

one line per sentence. fixed some typos

```suggestion
Digital emblems MUST identify the marked asset and their kind of digital emblem.
Beyond that, digital emblems MAY include other data, for example, an issuer or a validity window.
To accommodate use cases requiring extensible data, a digital emblem architecture SHOULD introduce minimal overhead size except for fields required to fulfill other requirements in this document. 
```
What does "without restriction" mean here?
What? How are we supposed to serve things via the DNS without Internet?

> What? How are we supposed to serve things via the DNS without Internet?

I didn't write this text, but if the root DNSSEC keys are cached or a known Issuer's root keys are cached (and still valid TTL) the validator could validate DNS records it receives that are signed with those keys.
I just see it as unnecessarily tying one hand to our backs. Are there any use cases that require offline validation? Why hasn't this been brought up so far?

Meaning the architecture doc cannot add restrictions of its own to which subsets of the emblem a particular digital emblem standards can validate or not. For example: the architecture doc cannot declare any given field unsignable, or must be signed, etc.

The extensibility I have been advocating for is was primarily to allow for new use cases, but I suppose there will be extensions to existing use cases over time as well.

In this text, it's unclear to me what is envisioned by "use cases requiring extensible data." Are we simply referring to fixed-size data or not? I've always assumed fixed-size is not possible for the use cases we've already discussed. Otherwise, I'm not clear on the definition here.

See [the mental model](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-rosenberg-diem-architecture/) I posted. I expect that each 'profile' will specify what data is signed. The dictionary fields may be self-describing of their types to make it more flexible.

Perhaps I'm just not following the intent here.
Sorry, Rohan, I pushed some changes that conflict with your suggestion. I will apply them locally now and push. Leaving this thread open for @ohmantics comments on the PR overall.

> I expect that each 'profile' will specify what data is signed.

That's exactly the intent of this text. The point is that the architecture cannot get in the way of "profiles" choosing to sign nothing, everything, or something. 

> it's unclear to me what is envisioned by "use cases requiring extensible data." 

Meaning any emblem profile that has any data defined beyond whatever the architecture considers to be core data format structure. Happy to rephrase though, I was just trying to highlight that the architecture must not get in the way of ay profile adding data and deciding whether to requried, allow, or forbid it being signed

If memory serves, the offline requirement was brought up in the first BoF for at least the WhiteFlag presentation and some of the goods/customs verification use.

Flipping the question around, why would we assume that is validator for EVERY type of digital emblem will always be online?

In what way would this tie our hands?

tl;dr: this along with other edits is intended to force the architecture to avoid adding constraints on individual digital emblem drafts for Deliverable 3. It doesn't add constraints on digital emblem drafts themselves. Digital emblem drafts who do require Internet connectivity can still do so. Draft that are special and don't want to don't have to. That's all.

So, just for the record, I'm not saying that the architecture document must use internet for validation.

If the architecture is reuired to allow for offline validation, that makes our life harder. It's another constraint. I would opt to not say anything about "is Internet connectivity required," and leave that for later documents to be figured out.

I am not aware of a use case that requires offline validation.

> If the architecture is reuired to allow for offline validation, that makes our life harder. 

Why? It should actually simplify the architecture doc because now it has to specify fewer things because it has every excuse to leave this to the emblem's specific draft rather than trying to define anything about validation that needs to be common across all digital emblems. For example, the architecture doc can complete this simply by saying something like:

> "**Optional Architecture Element \#\6: Validation.** Definitions of digital emblems need not define any validation mechanism. If they choose to do so, the document MUST define what the source(s) of trust will be, where the validator acquires them from or how the validator communicates with them, and what threat model is used to justify those choices."

Boom, done. Doesn't restrict to Internet access or assume either direction, farms out all details to each emblem as to how they do (or do not) implement Optional DE Architecture Element Number 6. Now we don't have to spend any WG time right now worrying about who wants this and why. If the WG wants to write a draft for an emblem that requires offline validation, cool. If they don't, cool. Neither emblem draft is harder to write based on this because they were always going to specify "I need to load trust anchors locally in case of intermittent loss of connectivity because latency matters" or "Internet connectivity is assumed, we don't support anything local" or whatever else.

```suggestion
To accommodate use cases requiring extensible data, a digital emblem architecture SHOULD introduce minimal overhead size except for fields required to fulfil other requirements in this document.
```
(a nit, fixing a minor typo)

I like the content of what is being added here, but think the sentence ordering for lines 205-207 is a bit confusing. I think something like the following might be a bit easier for the reader to understand.

```suggestion
Digital emblems MAY require validation but are in no way required to do so.
That is to say, some  use cases MAY use use unverified digital emblems.

To ensure extensibility, the digital emblem architecture MUST allow individual standards to support verification of all the digital emblem's data or of a defined subset thereof without restriction. This ensures digital emblems can support static or dynamic data without having to account for the pain of frequent re-signing of dynamic data whose validation is not required by a given digital emblem type.
```
(I would then remove line 207.)

comments.author: wnunezc
comments.body:
@felixlinker  @rohanmahy 

Could I ask for chair/editor guidance on the level of IHL terminology that is useful in this section?

I agree that the draft should use concise wording and should avoid creating new legal categories or new umbrella terms that could be misunderstood as normative.

At the same time, because this use case is directly connected to IHL, I think it is important that the terminology remains legally aligned. IHL is not limited only to one instrument. Depending on the use case, the relevant baseline may include the Geneva Conventions and their Additional Protocols, other treaty law, customary IHL, and other applicable rules for armed conflict.

Rohan’s suggestion to reference the original treaty or legal instrument defining a specific emblem or sign is helpful. My only concern is that the text should avoid implying that all IHL-related use cases derive from the same legal source or that DIEM is creating a new legal category.

Would the chairs/editors prefer this section to include a short terminology note, or should that be handled only through precise references in each use case?

For context, I am participating here in my personal capacity. I am a Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement volunteer and have been recognized as an IHL Ambassador attached to the Regional IHL Center for the Americas, after completing an IHL course and dissemination project.

I mention this only to explain the perspective from which I am reading these use cases: not as an attempt to speak on behalf of any institution, but as someone who is trying to help ensure that the technical use cases remain understandable and aligned with the applicable IHL background.

I would be glad to provide contextual input where the chairs or editors think that IHL background may be useful, while fully deferring to the WG on protocol scope, terminology, and technical design.

Adding a new term (Digital Protective Emblems is overkill - each use case is able to specify the specific requirement when they implement DEs, and how they will meet them,  so having two distinct types, the DE and the DPE, isn't necessary.  The four use cases  that received this new term are already well described here, and grouped already, so  the document giving them their own term is unnecessary as well as potentially confusing.

I think that the term Digital Protective Emblem for four use cases must not be added to the draft.  See a further comment in this review. I also think that the new reference to the Unesco Blue Shield document (which I read through) is misleading, because there are two differing statements  in there, on page 16. One list uses with digital assets, and  aligns with the ICRC, but further down the page is a second statement: "The digital Blue Shield emblem will also facilitate visualization on the UNESCO Sites Navigator of  cultural  properties".   This appears to allow for a digital emblem for a physical location because Sites Navigator is a GIS database. So the UNESCO reference should not be included or used as is.  These two concerns lead me to rejecting this PR.

reviews.author: rahelFain
reviews.body: The new terminology seems to open the door for the ITU to define technical standards for DPEs. In light and what I see as the addition of potentially misleading references, I cannot approve the suggested changes.

comments.body: The references here were used in the mailing list as a means of arguing that the ICRC's definition of digital emblems on the mailing list. In light of this and the potential issues with the ITU I also feel I need to reject these changes.

reviews.author: rohanmahy
reviews.body: I left some specific comments. If the scope of this is the emblems mentioned in the Geneva Conventions and its Additional Protocols, that should probably be the title of the section.

comments.body:
- I like spelling out the locations of the definition of the emblems (ex: AP I, GP and AP III), but I think the description of the physical emblem is distracting.
- the references should be to the original convention/treaty/law defining an emblem (GP, AP I, AP III, Hague Convention)

This would be way clearer

```suggestion
## Protective Emblems under The Geneva Conventions and its Additional Protocols
```

```suggestion
The Geneva Conventions and their Additional Protocols constitute the core of International Humanitarian Law (IHL).
```

This clarifies the distinction between other signs like a white flag and the UN flag and logo, and the four emblems mentioned in the GP+APs.

```suggestion
Some assets enjoy certain specific protections under IHL, including that they must not be attacked. In addition to recognizing other signs, IHL codifies four types of protective emblems for armed conflict, which inform other parties that marked assets benefit from one or several of these specific or special protections.
```

Defining a new capitalized term in a non-normative section of the document is simply not appropriate. Why not just say in line 325: 
"In context of the emblems above described in the GP..."?

Heya, agree and disagree at the same time. Agree: Listing the concrete laws is better and more accurate. Disagree: Blue shield is defined in the Hague Convention, so that's missing. Adding a third law/document/whatever to the text really becomes silly though.

As far as I'm aware, these emblems *are* the protective emblems under IHL, and that is how they are referred to, so I would leave it as is. The body of the section also is very accurate and clear about what we're referring to.
Why do you want to remove these references?

Without going into the gory details, International Humanitarian Law provides various levels of protection for a lot of things (ex: water treatment plants, schools), especially things that disproportionately impact civilians, that do not have explicit physical emblems defined. These rules are subtle. However if someone defined an emblem for schools, that Emblem would be intended to communicate this (limited) Protection under Internal Humanitarian Law. That is awfully close to the title. What is unique about the 4 emblems is that they are specifically called out as emblems in the Geneva Conventions + APs, and that 3 of them are defined there.

pulls.title: Response requirements
pulls.author: rahelFain
pulls.body:
Background: During IETF 125 it was pointed out that many of the digital emblem use cases seemed to assume that all queries for the digital emblem would always get the same response - which was not necessarily consistent with how DNS works. Further discussion about this highlighted that providing a proof of presence not only assumed that all queries would get the same response, but required that this be the case.
 This PR seeks to address that by requiring digital emblem use cases to specifically determine whether they require emblems to support assured response and/or consistent content in all responses and to detail the rationale for their determination.

reviews.author: felixlinker
reviews.body: Again, no strong opinion on these changes, but I see some technical issues.

comments.body: You first say that we must specify how responses are handled, but line 205 then comments on sending behavior. "Responsiveness and consistency" are about the sender, not the receiver.

I think this phrasing is hard to map to our current list of requirements/use cases. It is underspecified what a "request" is. Without defining that, you also cannot define consistency. Maybe, it would be easier to define consistency not w.r.t. to requests, but to assets? One could meaningfully say that "checking the emblem for asset X always results in the same emblems."

It also seems easier to me whether emblems should (or not) be publicly accessible.

This is a technical nit, but I disagree that consistency is required for proof of presence. As a strawman example, let's say that we use a public log to mark assets (Merkle hash tree style construction). For level 1, you could prove that an emblem was verifiable by referring to a public root hash and showing the correct proofs of inclusion (let's assume it is public information that the root hash was public at the relevant point in time). But it could be that there are multiple emblems in the log, and you may be allowed to show different emblems to different parties. Each emblem was verifiable, but parties may have had an inconsistent view of an assets emblems.

A lot of the unusual phrasing is due to the fact that digital emblems will be deployed via DNS.
DNS allows the deployment of multiple authoritative name servers across different geographical locations, and for them to point to different hosting or site servers. Additionally, an authoritative name server can employ specific logic to determine with which if any resolution response it replies to an incoming query.

Why not state "whether emblems should (or not) be publicly accessible"?
The phrasing you suggested does not account for the full range of ways an authoritative name server (or set of servers) could handle a search for emblem X. 
An example of an allowed behavior that wouldn't be accounted for: 
- responses can vary based on the geographic origin of the request: This could mean everyone who searches for emblem E in country/region X gets response A, whereas everyone who searches for the same emblem X in country Y (or simply outside of country/region X) gets response B. In that case both responses A and B could be said to be publicly accessible, but not necessarily consistent.

This may be a case where the specific phrasing of the requirement needs to shift to something more like proof of presence and visibility or proof of presence and accessibility. 
The basic premise is that anyone who checked for the emblem of asset S at point in time T should get the same emblem or response. That way, in the event of an alleged violation of the rules/standards/regulations denoted by a digital emblem there would be no doubt that had anyone checked the affected asset for an emblem before engaging, they would have seen it.

Why I had phrased it as the same response:
Part of what I have found tricky about the phrasing is that we have not defined which attributes of a response need to remain consistent for them to be considered the same digital emblem - since that is a discussion for the architecture. My instinct was to say that the responses served/fields controlled by the authoritative name server need to be identical to try and limit confusion. 
With that said, I can see how leaving the phrasing more flexible by simply saying they should be the same emblem - and waiting to define exactly what that means for now may be more appropriate.

I don't find the phrasing "unusual," I find it unclear. For "selective content," your suggestion of "publicly accessible" could work.

The rest I still find unclear. For example, what is an "emblem instance" and why would you query it? Are we querying emblems actively or are we querying for emblems bound to an identifier? I would assume the latter. Anyway, I think my suggestion would address different responses based on geography. I don't see what geography adds. Responses are the same or not, no?

OK I see how my phrasing (`how responses to queries...are handled`) made it sound like I was initially talking about the recipient. My intent was for both sentences to center on the sender and I will update the phrasing accordingly. Here's how I plan to rephrase it.
 `Specifications for each use case MUST each determine how servers must respond to queries for Digital Emblems of their specified type.
 Specifically , they must determine the responsiveness and consistency requirements for emblems of their given type and provide explanations of how the chosen requirements apply and rationales for their selection.`

@wnunezc These are all great suggestions, but I would argue that the specifics of what is the minimum content or representation of an emblem is a question that should be addressed in the architecture document rather than here. 
@felixlinker If there are multiple distinct Digital Emblems for a given asset (as will likely be denoted by a set keyword based domain name) they both need to be returned if consistent content is required. If two emblems are of the same type and ostensibly cover the same information/indicate the same protections/etc. it is likely that these are simply two versions of the same digital emblem.  To that end, I will be adding text stating that the exact definition for what constitutes a Digital Emblem (as well as a distinct one) will be defined in the architecture document.

This is super unclear, and it actually has nothing to do with requirements, which are clearly stated above. This sentence is a premature statement about the architecture. I'm going to just delete it. 

```suggestion
```

```suggestion
Note that as of this writing, neither the baseline definition for the minimum set of attributes that constitute a unique Digital
Emblem, nor the attributes needed to attain Consistent Content have been defined.
```

optional suggestion. @rahelFain Please either apply or reject; then merge this PR

pulls.title: Emblems requirements are distinct; Future proofing Section 3.1.1
pulls.author: rohanmahy
pulls.body:
This proposed text from Sarah (as a chair) intends to clarify that each emblem type has distinct freedom with respect to other emblem types, and also to future proof the work of the DIEM WG.  
@sarahjennings-ietf

comments.body: I have a hard time understanding what "applying an emblem" really means here.

I understand “applying an emblem” in the physical world as placing a visible protective sign on a person, object, facility, vehicle, building, or infrastructure so that third parties can recognize, often without announcing themselves, that the marked bearer is claiming a specific protected status under an applicable legal framework.

For example, in the physical world an emblem may be painted on a building, displayed on a flag, marked on a vehicle, or placed on equipment. The point is visibility and recognition: an observer can see the sign and understand that the marked object or infrastructure is associated with a protected category.

The emblem itself does not create the legal protection. The legal protection comes from the applicable treaty, convention, or legal rule. The emblem makes that protection visible and helps others identify the object or infrastructure that must be respected, provided the emblem is used lawfully.

Examples of physical protective signs/emblems include:

- Red Cross, Red Crescent, and Red Crystal: identify protected medical services, medical units, medical transports, and authorized humanitarian actors.
- Civil Defence distinctive sign: an equilateral blue triangle on an orange ground, used for civil defence organizations, personnel, buildings, material, and civilian shelters.
- Blue Shield: identifies protected cultural property, such as museums, archives, monuments, libraries, and heritage sites.
- Special sign for works and installations containing dangerous forces: three bright orange circles on the same axis, used for dams, dykes, and nuclear electrical generating stations.

In the DIEM context, I understand the goal as translating this physical concept into the digital environment. Instead of painting a symbol on a building or raising a flag, a digital emblem would be bound to a digital asset, service, domain, data system, or infrastructure component in a way that allows DIEM-aware clients to discover, validate, and interpret the claimed protected status.

In practice, parties to an armed conflict, especially States with trained armed forces and legal advisers, are already expected to recognize and interpret these physical signs under IHL. A similar expectation could emerge in the digital environment: technical tools, operators, CERT/CSIRT teams, military cyber units, and other DIEM-aware actors should be able to recognize and validate digital markings in a standardized way, rather than relying on ad hoc or hidden signals.

So, in this context, “applying an emblem” could mean binding a digital emblem or marking to a specific asset through the mechanisms defined by the relevant DIEM profile, while preserving the distinction between:

- the visual or digital marker;
- the underlying legal/protected status;
- the authorization model;
- and the validation process.

I think there's a tension between the words "use case" and "emblem."

In the first sentence, "use case" and "emblem" seem to refer to the same thing. Example: Use case is emblems of type X, the use case says emblems of type X can only be applied to Y. Makes sense.

In the second sentence, "future use cases" seem to be different to "previously defined use cases," such as emblems of type X in my previous example. Say, a feature use case pops up of emblems of type Z. This use case now ought to "use that emblem (of type X) under a[n] [...] expanded domain of application." Doesn't make much sense to me - why would emblem of type Z use emblems of type X? Or are you referring to standards in the second sentence and not logical types of emblems?

> @felixlinker is this better?

Possible wording:

“The requirements for individual digital emblem types are independent. Requirements defined for one emblem type MUST NOT constrain, override, or otherwise affect the requirements, design, or use of any other digital emblem type.

A use case describes a context of use. An emblem type identifies the kind of emblem, distinctive sign, or marking being represented. Where a use case specifies a limited domain of application for a particular emblem type, such limitation SHOULD be understood as reflecting the constraints of that use case only.

It SHOULD NOT be interpreted as preventing future use cases from defining a different or expanded domain of application for the same emblem type, provided that the emblem type’s core semantics remain intact.”
retracting change

pulls.title: Add Other IHL use cases section
pulls.author: rohanmahy
pulls.body:
This covers:
-  humanitarian medical NGOs that have protection but that traditionally do not use Red C* physical emblems.
- marking civilian infrastructure which has qualified protection under IHL.

comments.author: wnunezc
comments.body:
Thank you for opening this PR. I think this section is very important because it helps clarify that DIEM may need to address IHL-related use cases beyond the traditional Red Cross / Red Crescent / Red Crystal emblem context.

I would like to support the general direction of this PR, especially the distinction between:

1. humanitarian or medical actors/assets that may be protected under IHL but do not traditionally use the Red Cross, Red Crescent, or Red Crystal physical emblems; and

2. civilian infrastructure that may have qualified protection under IHL.

From a humanitarian law and technical architecture perspective, I think this distinction is important because not all IHL-protected civilian objects are humanitarian or medical assets.

For example, civilian water systems, energy infrastructure, food supply chains, civilian telecommunications, dams, and other services indispensable to the civilian population may be protected or specially relevant under IHL. However, these assets should not be semantically merged with humanitarian/medical digital emblem profiles, because doing so could dilute or confuse the protective meaning of the existing distinctive emblems.

It may be useful for the draft to make this profile separation explicit.

A possible framing could be:

- Digital humanitarian/medical emblem profiles: for protected medical units, services, and humanitarian organizations, especially where the logic of the existing distinctive emblems is directly relevant.
- Other IHL-protected civilian infrastructure profiles: for civilian objects and services that may be protected or specially relevant under IHL, but should not reuse or be confused with humanitarian/medical emblem profiles.
- Sector-specific protected-status profiles: for categories such as water, energy, food supply, dams, civilian communications, or other legally and operationally justified cases.

This may also help with architectural extensibility. A common DIEM discovery and validation architecture could support different profiles, while preserving clear semantic separation between humanitarian/medical emblems and other protected-status signals.

This distinction could also be relevant to the idea of a minimal presence indicator. For humanitarian/medical assets, a presence indicator could trigger validation of a Digital Emblem object. For other IHL-protected civilian infrastructure, a similar technical mechanism might trigger validation of a different protected-status profile, without calling it a humanitarian emblem and without reusing the same legal or symbolic meaning.

In short, I think the draft could benefit from explicitly stating that:

- DIEM should avoid overloading humanitarian/medical emblems with all IHL-protected civilian infrastructure use cases;
- the architecture should remain extensible enough to support clearly separated profiles where appropriate; and
- any future non-humanitarian protected-status profiles should be carefully defined to avoid misuse, overbroad claims, or weakening of the protective value of the distinctive emblems.

This is not intended to expand the scope indiscriminately, but rather to preserve clarity between different IHL-related categories while keeping the architecture flexible enough for future profile-specific work.

pulls.title: Clarify validation and downstream interpretation boundary
pulls.author: wnunezc
pulls.body:
This PR adds a short non-normative clarification about the boundary between DIEM technical validation and downstream interpretation.

Recent mailing-list and GitHub discussions have highlighted the need to distinguish between:

- discovering digital emblem data associated with an asset;
- technically validating returned data according to DIEM mechanisms; and
- interpreting downstream legal, operational, or policy consequences.

The proposed text does not change any protocol requirement. It only clarifies that a successful DIEM validation result is a technical result and does not, by itself, determine downstream legal or operational consequences.

This is intended to help keep the draft focused on discovery and technical validation, while leaving downstream interpretation to the relevant user community, applicable framework, competent authorities, and operational processes.

Thank you, Rohan.

I revised the PR to remove the broad downstream-interpretation text and moved the clarification to the Validation section.

I also removed “technically valid” and tried to express the narrower point: validation establishes only the properties defined by the relevant digital emblem type, and does not by itself establish that all asserted emblem data is factually true or correct unless that property is explicitly required.
